content.title: 我叫... — My name is...
content.body:
**我叫...** (Wǒ jiào...) is the standard pattern for introducing your name in Mandarin.

**Literal meaning:** "I am-called..."

**Usage:** Simply add your name after 叫.

Examples:
- 我叫大卫。(Wǒ jiào Dàwèi.) — My name is David.
- 我叫小李。(Wǒ jiào Xiǎo Lǐ.) — My name is Xiao Li.

content.title: Creating Your Own Introduction
content.body:
In Chinese culture, first introductions typically follow a structured pattern. When meeting someone professionally or in a formal setting, you should be prepared to introduce yourself clearly.

**Key elements of a proper introduction:**
1. Greeting: 你好！
2. Name: 我叫...
3. Identity: 我是... (nationality

content.title: 你叫什么名字？— What is your name?
content.body:
The phrase **你叫什么名字？** (Nǐ jiào shénme míngzi?) is the standard way to ask someone's name in Mandarin.

**Literal meaning:** "You are-called what name?"

**Structure breakdown:**
- 你 (nǐ) = you
- 叫 (jiào) = are called
- 什么 (shénme) = what
- 名字 (míngzi) = name

This question follows Chinese word orde

content.title: Introducing Yourself Completely
content.body:
When introducing yourself in Mandarin, you typically provide both your name and nationality/role. The two main patterns are combined:

**Pattern 1: Name**
我叫 + [name]

**Pattern 2: Identity**
我是 + [nationality/role]

**Complete introduction example:**
- 我叫大卫。我是美国人。
- (Wǒ jiào Dàwèi. Wǒ shì Měiguó ré

content.title: Expanding the Conversation
content.body:
After basic introductions, conversations naturally expand. Here are common follow-up patterns:

**Asking follow-up questions:**
- 你也是学生吗？(Nǐ yě shì xuésheng ma?) — Are you also a student?
- 你是老师吗？(Nǐ shì lǎoshī ma?) — Are you a teacher?

**Responding with 也 (also):**
- 我也是学生。(Wǒ yě shì xuésheng.) —

content.title: Common Mistakes in Introductions
content.body:
Learners often make several common mistakes when introducing themselves in Chinese:

**Mistake 1: Wrong pattern for introducing your name**
- ❌ 我是大卫 (when first introducing yourself)
- ✅ 我叫大卫 (standard for introductions)
- ℹ️ Note: Both are grammatically correct but serve different functions — 我叫 in

content.title: 什么 (shénme) — what
content.body:
**什么** (shénme) is the question word for "what" in Mandarin Chinese.

**Pronunciation:** shénme (shén = second tone rising, me = neutral tone)

**Usage:** 什么 is used to ask about things, names, or identities. In questions, it appears where the answer would go in a statement.

Example:
- 你叫什么名字？(Nǐ j

content.title: 名字 (míngzi) — name
content.body:
**名字** (míngzi) means "name" in Mandarin Chinese.

**Pronunciation:** míngzi (míng = second tone rising, zi = neutral tone)

**Usage:** 名字 is a noun referring to a person's name.

Example:
- 你的名字很好听。(Nǐ de míngzi hěn hǎotīng.) — Your name sounds very nice.

content.title: Question Word Placement
content.body:
In Mandarin Chinese, question words like **什么** (what) and **哪** (which) stay in the position where the answer would appear. They do not move to the beginning of the sentence like in English.

**Pattern:** Question words replace the information being asked about

**Example with 什么:**
- Statement: 我叫

content.title: First Meeting Dialogue
content.body:
**Read this dialogue between two people meeting for the first time:**

李明: 你好！
大卫: 你好！
李明: 你叫什么名字？
大卫: 我叫大卫。我是美国人。你呢？
李明: 我叫李明。我是中国人。
大卫: 认识你很高兴！
李明: 我也很高兴！

**Vocabulary note:**
- 你呢？(Nǐ ne?) — And you? / How about you?
- 我也 (wǒ yě) — I also / Me too

content.title: 我 (wǒ) — I, me
content.body:
The character **我** (wǒ) means "I" or "me" in Mandarin Chinese. It is one of the most fundamental pronouns you will use.

**Pronunciation:** wǒ (third tone — low-dipping tone: starts mid-low, dips to low, then rises slightly (in isolation); in speech often a low tone)

**Usage:** 我 is used as the su

content.title: 哪 (nǎ) — which
content.body:
**哪** (nǎ) is the question word for "which" in Mandarin Chinese.

**Pronunciation:** nǎ (third tone — falling then rising)

**Usage:** 哪 is used to ask about choices or options, often combined with other words to form question phrases.

Example:
- 哪国人 (nǎ guó rén) — which country person = what natio

content.title: Asking About Someone's Identity
content.body:
When meeting someone new in Chinese, you can ask both about their name and nationality using two key question patterns:

**Question 1: About name**
你叫什么名字？(Nǐ jiào shénme míngzi?)

**Question 2: About nationality**
你是哪国人？(Nǐ shì nǎ guó rén?)

**Conversation example:**
- A: 你叫什么名字？(What is your name?

content.title: 认识 (rènshi) — to know, to recognize, to meet
content.body:
**认识** (rènshi) means "to know," "to recognize," or "to meet" (someone) in Mandarin.

**Pronunciation:** rènshi (rèn = fourth tone falling, shi = neutral tone)

**Usage:** 认识 is used specifically for knowing people, not facts or how to do things.

Example:
- 认识你很高兴。(Rènshi nǐ hěn gāoxìng.) — Nice to

content.title: Difference Between 叫 and 是
content.body:
Both **叫** (jiào) and **是** (shì) can be used in introductions, but they serve different purposes:

**叫 (jiào)** = "to be called/named"
- Used specifically for names
- Pattern: 我叫 + name
- Example: 我叫李明。(My name is Li Ming.)

**是 (shì)** = "to be"
- Used for identity, nationality, profession
- Patte

content.title: Nationalities and Countries
content.body:
In Chinese, nationalities are formed by adding **人** (rén - person) after the country name.

**Pattern:** [Country name] + 人

**Common examples:**
- 中国人 (Zhōngguó rén) — Chinese person
- 美国人 (Měiguó rén) — American
- 英国人 (Yīngguó rén) — British person
- 法国人 (Fǎguó rén) — French person
- 日本人 (Rìběn r

content.title: 国 (guó) — country
content.body:
**国** (guó) means "country" or "nation" in Mandarin Chinese.

**Pronunciation:** guó (second tone — rising)

**Usage:** 国 is commonly used in words related to nationality and countries.

Examples:
- 中国 (Zhōngguó) — China (Middle Country)
- 美国 (Měiguó) — America (Beautiful Country)
- 英国 (Yīngguó) — E

content.title: 我是... — I am... (nationality/role)
content.body:
**我是...** (Wǒ shì...) is used to state your identity, including nationality, profession, or role.

**Structure:** 我 + 是 + [noun/noun phrase]

Examples:
- 我是美国人。(Wǒ shì Měiguó rén.) — I am American.
- 我是学生。(Wǒ shì xuésheng.) — I am a student.
- 我是老师。(Wǒ shì lǎoshī.) — I am a teacher.

content.title: 你 (nǐ) — you
content.body:
The character **你** (nǐ) means "you" in Mandarin Chinese. 你 is the standard form for addressing peers and equals; use 您 (nín) for formal or respectful address.

**Pronunciation:** nǐ (third tone — falling then rising)

**Usage:** 你 is the subject pronoun for the person you are speaking to.

Example:

content.title: 你是哪国人？— What is your nationality?
content.body:
**你是哪国人？** (Nǐ shì nǎ guó rén?) is the standard way to ask about someone's nationality in Mandarin.

**Literal meaning:** "You are which country person?"

**Structure breakdown:**
- 你 (nǐ) = you
- 是 (shì) = are
- 哪 (nǎ) = which
- 国 (guó) = country
- 人 (rén) = person

content.title: Cultural Context of Chinese Introductions
content.body:
Chinese introductions often differ from Western customs in several ways:

**Information sharing:** Chinese speakers typically share more personal information early in introductions, including hometown, work unit (单位 dānwèi), or school. This helps establish social context and potential connections.

content.title: 高兴 (gāoxìng) — happy, pleased, glad
content.body:
**高兴** (gāoxìng) means "happy," "pleased," or "glad" in Mandarin.

**Pronunciation:** gāoxìng (gāo = first tone high level, xìng = fourth tone falling)

**Usage:** 高兴 describes a state of happiness or pleasure.

Example:
- 我很高兴。(Wǒ hěn gāoxìng.) — I am very happy.

content.title: 人 (rén) — person, people
content.body:
**人** (rén) means "person" or "people" in Mandarin Chinese.

**Pronunciation:** rén (second tone — rising)

**Usage:** 人 is used to refer to people in general, and is often combined with other words to indicate nationality or type of person.

Examples:
- 中国人 (Zhōngguó rén) — Chinese person
- 美国人 (Mě

content.title: 叫 (jiào) — to be called/named
content.body:
The verb **叫** (jiào) means "to be called" or "to be named." It is the most common way to express names in Chinese.

**Pronunciation:** jiào (fourth tone — sharp falling)

**Usage:** 叫 is used with 我 to introduce your name: 我叫... (I am called...)

Example:
- 我叫李明。(Wǒ jiào Lǐ Míng.) — My name is Li M

content.title: Responding to Introductions
content.body:
After someone introduces themselves, it's polite to respond with **认识你很高兴** (Rènshi nǐ hěn gāoxìng) — "Nice to meet you."

**Full introduction exchange example:**

Person A:
- 你好！我叫王明。我是中国人。
- (Hello! My name is Wang Ming. I am Chinese.)

Person B:
- 认识你很高兴！我叫大卫。我是美国人。
- (Nice to meet you! My name i

content.title: 认识你很高兴 — Nice to meet you
content.body:
**认识你很高兴** (Rènshi nǐ hěn gāoxìng) is a polite phrase meaning "Nice to meet you."

**Literal meaning:** "Knowing you very happy" = "I'm very happy to meet you"

**Structure breakdown:**
- 认识 (rènshi) = to know/meet
- 你 (nǐ) = you
- 很 (hěn) = very
- 高兴 (gāoxìng) = happy/pleased

This phrase is used w

content.title: 是 (shì) — to be (am/is/are)
content.body:
**是** (shì) is the verb "to be" in Mandarin Chinese, equivalent to "am," "is," or "are" in English.

**Pronunciation:** shì (fourth tone — sharp falling)

**Usage:** 是 is used to link the subject with a noun or noun phrase that defines or identifies it.

Examples:
- 我是学生。(Wǒ shì xuésheng.) — I am a

content.title: 很 (hěn) — very
content.body:
**很** (hěn) means "very" in Mandarin Chinese.

**Pronunciation:** hěn (third tone — falling then rising)

**Usage:** 很 is placed before adjectives to intensify them. In Chinese, it is often used even when "very" is not emphasized in English.

Example:
- 很高兴 (hěn gāoxìng) — very happy/pleased
